scenario.title: BCC vs MC/DC Showdown
scenario.narrative: We return to the Autonomous Brake trial — the same 4-condition decision that needed 16 BCC tests but blew the court's 3-log budget. Armed with MC/DC, you can win the case with just N+1 = 5 tests. Build a minimum suite that proves independent effect for all four conditions.
scenario.code:
if obstacle and spd50 and dry and no_brk:
    trigger_brake()

conditions.label: Driver did not press the manual brake

claim: For a pure 4-input AND like this one, an MC/DC suite can be built with exactly N+1 = 5 tests. Pick the all-TRUE 'base' case plus 4 single-flip variants — each one falsifying exactly one condition — to prove independent effect for every variable.
charges: Select EXACTLY 5 rows from the 16-row truth table that form valid independence pairs for ALL four conditions.
Strategy hint: an AND chain has a unique TRUE row (everything TRUE). Pair it with four single-flip neighbours — each of which sets exactly one variable to FALSE — to isolate each condition.
Each chosen row must be reusable: the all-TRUE row will partner FOUR different pairs.
correct_answer_explanation:
Verdict: brakes proven faulty with 5 logs out of 16. Here is how the suite interlocks around the all-TRUE row R1:

• **Obstacle independence: R1 ↔ R9** — only obstacle flips (T→F); spd50, dry, no_brk all stay TRUE; outcome flips T→F.
• **Speed independence: R1 ↔ R5** — only spd50 flips (T→F); the others stay TRUE; outcome flips T→F.
• **Dry independence: R1 ↔ R3** — only dry flips (T→F); the others stay TRUE; outcome flips T→F.
• **No-Override independence: R1 ↔ R2** — only no_brk flips (T→F); the others stay TRUE; outcome flips T→F.

R1 partners all four pairs — that single reuse is what brings the count from 2^4 = 16 BCC tests down to N+1 = 5 MC/DC tests, a 69% reduction with the same fault-detection guarantees. This is why DO-178C aviation software and ISO 26262 automotive safety mandate MC/DC.
wrong_answer_explanation: Those 5 rows don't form valid MC/DC pairs for all four variables. Strategy: start from R1 (everything TRUE → outcome TRUE). Then pick the four rows that flip EXACTLY ONE variable to FALSE (R2, R3, R5, R9). Each pairs with R1 to prove that variable's independent effect.
hints: Strategy: this is a pure AND chain, so the only TRUE outcome is the all-TRUE row. That row (R1) becomes the shared partner for every condition's independence pair.
From R1, find the FOUR rows that flip exactly one variable to FALSE: R9 flips only obstacle, R5 flips only spd50, R3 flips only dry, R2 flips only no_brk. Each pair with R1 isolates one variable.
Worked solution: pick R1, R2, R3, R5, R9. Pairs: obstacle via R1↔R9, speed via R1↔R5, dry via R1↔R3, no_brk via R1↔R2. R1 is reused four times — N+1 in action.

misconceptions.explanation_md: **MC/DC vs BCC** — both are strong combination-coverage criteria, but they scale very differently. BCC requires every cell of the 2^N truth table (16 tests for N=4, 1,024 for N=10). MC/DC requires only N+1 to 2N tests (5 for N=4, 11–20 for N=10) while still guaranteeing that every condition is shown to independently affect the decision. That is why MC/DC is the **gold standard** for safety-critical software in DO-178C (avionics) and ISO 26262 (automotive).
